title: Collections & Data Structures
subtitle: Built-in batteries: list, dict, set, tuple

sections.title: Your java.util Map, Drastically Simplified
sections.content:
list
ArrayList
ordered, mutable
dict
HashMap
insertion-ordered
set
HashSet
unique, unordered
tuple
List.of(...)
immutable, fixed

sections.java: // Generics + factory methods List<String> names = new ArrayList<>(); names.add("Ada"); names.add("Linus"); String first = names.get(0); int n = names.size();
sections.python: # Literal syntax, no generics needed names: list[str] = ["Ada", "Linus"] names.append("Grace") first = names[0] last = names[-1] # negative indexing! n = len(names) # len() not .size()

sections.title: Slicing Beats Loops
sections.content: Python's slice syntax seq[start:stop:step] replaces dozens of subList / loop patterns. names[1:3] is a sublist, names[::-1] reverses, names[::2] takes every other. Slices work on lists, strings, and tuples uniformly — there's no equivalent one-liner in Java.

sections.java: // HashMap with explicit generics Map<String, Integer> ages = new HashMap<>(); ages.put("Ada", 36); Integer a = ages.get("Ada"); int safe = ages.getOrDefault("Bob", 0); if (ages.containsKey("Ada")) { }
sections.python: # dict literal with {} ages: dict[str, int] = {"Ada": 36} ages["Linus"] = 54 a = ages["Ada"] # KeyError if absent safe = ages.get("Bob", 0) # default if "Ada" in ages: # in operator ...

sections.title: dict[key] Throws — Use .get() Like getOrDefault
sections.content: ages["missing"] raises KeyError, it does not return null the way map.get() does in Java. The Java getOrDefault equivalent is ages.get("missing", default). And membership is tested with the in operator, not .containsKey().

sections.java: // Immutable view / unmodifiable List<Integer> point = List.of(3, 4); // no real tuple — must make a class or array int[] coords = {3, 4};
sections.python: # tuple = immutable, fixed-size record point = (3, 4) x, y = point # unpacking! # Great for returning multiple values def size() -> tuple[int, int]: return 1920, 1080

sections.title: Tuple Unpacking & Multiple Returns
sections.content: Tuples let a function return several values cleanly: w, h = size(). No need for an out-param, a wrapper class, or a Pair. Swap two variables with a, b = b, a — no temp. This idiom shows up constantly in Python.

sections.java: // Aliasing: both refer to same list List<Integer> a = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2)); List<Integer> b = a; b.add(3); // a is now [1, 2, 3] too — same object
sections.python: # Same trap — assignment never copies a = [1, 2] b = a # alias, NOT a copy b.append(3) # a is now [1, 2, 3] too c = a.copy() # shallow copy when you d = a[:] # actually want one

sections.question: After `a = [1, 2]; b = a; b.append(3)`, what is `a`?
options: [1, 2, 3] — b is another name for the same list
Raises an error — lists are immutable
sections.explanation: Just like assigning one Java List reference to another, b = a makes both names point at the same list object — it never copies. Mutating through b is visible through a. To get an independent list use a.copy() or a[:].
